title: Dog Licked Antifreeze: Emergency Triage Guide & Next Steps
meta_desc: If your dog licked antifreeze, this is a life-threatening emergency. Learn why immediate veterinary care is vital and how to help your pet now.
content_html:
What is Antifreeze Poisoning?

Antifreeze typically contains ethylene glycol, a chemical that is extremely toxic to dogs even in minute quantities. Because it has a sweet taste, dogs are often tempted to lick spills. Once ingested, the substance is rapidly absorbed by the body and processed by the liver into toxic metabolites that cause sudden, severe kidney failure.

Is it an Emergency?

Yes. This is a critical, high-level medical emergency. There is no safe amount of antifreeze for a dog to ingest; even a few licks from a garage floor can be fatal. Immediate veterinary intervention is the only way to prevent permanent organ damage or death. You must seek help even if your dog appears fine, as internal damage begins long before outward symptoms show.

Why a Photo of the Product Helps

If it is safe to do so, quickly snap a photo of the antifreeze container or the spill. Different brands use different chemical bases, such as ethylene glycol or the slightly less toxic propylene glycol. Showing your veterinarian the exact brand and ingredient list helps them determine the specific toxicity level and choose the most effective treatment protocol for your dog immediately.

faqs.question: How much antifreeze is considered toxic to a dog?
faqs.answer: For a medium-sized dog, as little as one tablespoon of ethylene glycol-based antifreeze can be fatal. Because the margin for safety is zero, any suspected ingestion must be treated as a crisis.

faqs.question: What are the early signs of antifreeze poisoning?
faqs.answer: Within 30 minutes to 12 hours, dogs may appear 'drunk,' showing signs of staggering, lack of coordination, excessive thirst, or vomiting. This is the most critical window for treatment.

faqs.question: Should I try to make my dog vomit at home?
faqs.answer: Do not induce vomiting unless specifically instructed to do so by a veterinarian or a pet poison control expert. In some cases, it may not be safe or may delay life-saving clinical treatment.
